title: What Really Happens When You Stop Chasing What Doesn't Matter
description: In this video, Sandeep draws on his journey as a monk, MIT graduate, and multi-millionaire CEO to share five life-changing lessons on success, fulfillment, and meaning. Through powerful stories, he reveals why visible wealth isn't always true value, how a growth mindset can become a trap, and why mastering today is more important than chasing tomorrow. Sandeep also unpacks the art of giving feedback that connects instead of criticizes, and shows how curiosity becomes the ultimate compass when plans fall apart. This is a blueprint for anyone chasing growth but searching for something deeper.
transcript:
You know, in life, we chase everything
except what matters. We sprint toward
goals, then move the finish line. We
live to work for getting what we're
working for. I have lived both extremes.
Monk and CEO, homeless and financially
free, fired and building billion-dollar
companies. And along the way, a handful
of advice quietly changed how I saw the
game and how I lived my life. It might
do the same for you, too. Let's get into
it. Here's the first life-changing
advice I got. The fastest way to your
dream job is to slow down and master the
one you already have. You can't leap
into the next role by sleepwalking
through the current one. Years ago, I
was sitting through my annual review and
my boss asked me about my long-term
plans and I told her I wanted to rise up
to a sea level position someday to
serve, to lead, to have a broader
impact. And I asked her how to get there
because she was the president of the
company. She said three words that
changed everything. Focus on today. That
was it. I was a little surprised, a
little humbled, no 5-year plan, no
networking road map. Her advice was
simple but stellar. Be great exactly
where your feet are. But most of us
don't focus on that. We treat our
current job like a waiting room, don't
we? The goal is always to catch the next
train. How do you become more valuable
in the future if you're not creating
value right now? So, here are a few
things you can try. Solve what others
ignore. Start small. What are the things
your team needs help on? What irritates
your boss? Pick one thing. Fix one
thing. Do it quietly. Do it
thoughtfully. Do it skillfully. You
know, use artificial intelligence. Use
emotional intelligence. Use all the
intelligence you have, but use it here.
Use it now at your current job because
your next role won't come from your
resume. It'll come from your reputation.
Master what's in front of you in your
present. The future has a way of
noticing that. Second advice, not
everything that's visible is valuable.
Being rich in money but poor in time
isn't prosperity, it's prison. I got
this advice from a CEO who was running a
multi-billion dollar company. He wasn't
a tech company. He was already richer
than his younger self would have ever
imagined. So I kept asking him when was
he planning to slow down, but he wanted
more. He wanted the big B, billions.
Then one day the company stumbled and
billions of dollars just evaporated
overnight. If he had cashed out before,
he could have walked away with hundreds
of millions of dollars. but instead he
walked away with almost nothing.
Months later, we met for drinks and he
shared something that was heartbreaking.
He said, "You know, everyone hates me
now. I've lost all my friends except the
two I had in college. My advice to you,
Sandep, once you have what you need,
stop chasing for more."
He was right. If you've already built a
life you like, stop mortgaging it for a
bigger one. What's the point of winning
if it cost you everyone who mattered? We
chase what we see because everyone else
can see it too, right? Our bank balance,
our fancy cars, our job titles, easy to
count, easy to flex. That's why we all
fall for it. You know, a couple of years
ago, Deote did an interesting study and
they found that 70% of high earners
report feeling time starved. Seven out
of 10. You may end up with success and
money, but that won't buy you time or
freedom if you don't make a conscious
choice to prioritize those ideas. And
here's something worth trying. Really
ask yourself three questions. Number
one, how much time do I spend with those
I love? Number two, how often do I laugh
with my spouse, with my kids, with my
boyfriend, my girlfriend? Number three,
how many days a week do I stop to feel
gratitude and joy? Those are good
questions to ask. The third advice came
to me through a painful experience. Even
the right words can blow up
relationships if you say them at the
wrong time. Way back when I served as
the CEO of a great tech firm, I used to
think good feedback meant precision,
clarity, efficiency. So I would drop
dozens and dozens of comments on our
board deck that our team was working on
over the weekend. You know, in my head I
was being helpful, strategic, and
thoughtful, but to the team it felt like
an ambush. One day my CFO cornered me
like pulled me aside in front of the
elevator and he said, "You know, when
you send so many comments, I appreciate
it. is very helpful, but sometimes it
feels like we've already lost before the
game even began. And I just looked at
him and I said, "Wow, you're absolutely
right. I wish someone had told me this
before." Because I thought I was
coaching, but coaching doesn't work when
it's dropped like a grenade and Google
Slides 48 hours before high stakes
meeting. And this is the challenge of
our time for all of us. As more and more
teams get spread out in different
locations, it's harder to provide
feedback that lands the way you
intended. And the truth is this is not
just a workplace issue, right? It's a
human challenge. We do the same thing to
our spouse, kids, friends, and
co-founders. We try to be helpful. But
when feedback lacks context, tone,
timing, it doesn't feel like support,
does it? It feels like criticism. And
that's the balance we all have to
strike. Feedback is like medicine. It
only works when delivered in the right
dose at the right time through the right
system. People don't want to be judged.
They want to be seen. They want to know
that their efforts mattered, that they
mattered, even if the results may miss
the mark. That's why I now think about
building what I call a trust loop before
I give feedback. I'm still not good at
it. I'm constantly working on it and I
still make mistakes, but it's a simple
five-step framework that helps ensure
feedback lands as intended,
constructive, not critical. First, say
it in person or face to face. Don't
coach through comments or Slack threads.
There's no replacement for human
connection. Written comments, no matter
how carefully worded, can't replicate
tone and empathy and nuance. Second
step, affirm. Start with what's working,
not just as a tactic. Like don't do
what's known in business as the sandwich
where you start with good, then you
insert all the bad stuff and end with
something good again. No, that's not
what I'm talking about. This is about
giving real specific recognition for
effort and progress. Third step,
clarify. Ask questions before offering
solutions. Understand what's behind the
issue. Why is it stuck? And what's
happening from their perspective?
Feedback from awareness is far more
effective than feedback from assumption.
Fourth, give feedback in private, not in
public. You know, feedback doesn't need
a stage. It needs a soft landing. Public
corrections rarely lead to growth. They
lead to embarrassment, shutdown,
resentment. Why do that? And fifth, have
a real conversation, not a commentary.
Sit down or go for a walk. Have a real
talk. Make it safe to have a dialogue,
not a monologue. I have learned one
thing after spending decades in sea
level positions. People don't change
because they are corrected. They change
because they feel understood. When they
feel safe, they open up. When they feel
judged, they shut down. So always
remember, correction without connection
will always sound like rejection. The
fourth advice I got was this. The growth
mindset makes us successful, but it can
also make us restless. There was a time
in Mumbai when I was homeless. I was
lost. So when I had the opportunity to
restart my life in Boston and in New
York, I of course poured every waking
moment in my 20s and 30s into working
and learning and building my career. It
made sense, right? The plan was simple.
Grind now, enjoy later. But now when I
look back, I realize what was actually
happening. It was grind now and grind
later because I got really lucky and by
my late 30s I had hit my number. I
finally had financial freedom but my
nervous system hadn't accepted it. So I
kept going. Maybe it was the scarcity
mentality from my childhood. Who knows?
But I remember feeling like I was an
unfinished work of art that would expire
before it was finished. I was still a
hamster, just on a different wheel. The
wheel gets shinier and it spins faster
and we just keep going on. We get there
and then we move the goalpost. The
problem here isn't ambition. It's this
idea that fulfillment is one more
achievement away. That one last hit,
that one last score, and then I'll ride
into the sunset. But there's nothing in
that future that will finally fix it
all. Psychologist Kristen Nef calls it
lack of self-compassion. When our
self-worth depends on being superior, it
becomes fragile and we stay stuck on
this treadmill of endless comparison.
That sounds familiar to me. Of course,
you should never stop striving to become
better. That's not the solution. But you
also need to start enjoying the idea of
being who you are in parallel to find
that balance between being and becoming.
Here are three questions to ask yourself
perhaps every week. What did I learn?
It's about growth. What did I improve?
It's about others. And the most
important question, what did I smile
about today? And now for the last advice
I received. When your plans fall apart,
curiosity is the only compass that still
works. My music teacher, a great
maestro, changed how I thought about
curiosity versus certainty. I remember
sitting down for a lesson one of those
days and he was relaxing on his couch
and I was sitting in front of him and I
was all like warmed up, ready to nail
whatever comes my way. But he stopped me
cold and he asked me, "What is your
instrument telling you today?" It wasn't
some composition he was talking about,
not some technique. He was teaching me
how to listen, how to be curious about
my own instrument, about my own ideas,
my own instinct, to give up on certainty
in favor of curiosity. That was a
phenomenal advice because most of us
think success requires a master plan,
right? A 5-year strategy with mapped out
milestones and oh, you got to write all
the details down and you got to
visualize it every day. All of that is
important. I'm not downplaying any of
it. But the best opportunities don't
announce themselves. They surprise us.
They show up as some weird side project
or a random conversation or an
accidental interaction from the left
field that we never expected. Life isn't
a chessboard. There are no guaranteed
moves. It's closer to surfing. You don't
control the waves. You may have a
perfect plan to surf, but when the wind
starts shifting and the waves disappear,
the plan isn't going to matter. All you
can do is stay curious, stay open, stay
tuned in and wait for the next wave.
Curiosity is like a whisper. Our mind
has to be quiet enough to hear its
voice. So each week ask yourself, what
am I curious about? Then follow up on
that thread. Read one article outside of
your field or ask that one question you
haven't asked before or even try one
project that doesn't have a clear ROI.
Let randomness surprise you. Remember,
the best opportunities don't knock. They
vanish as soon as they appear. But
curiosity
knows where to find them. If you found
this video helpful, hey, please
subscribe. Also watch the video about
seven things that you can stop doing in
order to find financial freedom and
fulfillment. I appreciate all your time,
all the comments, all the love. Thank
you and I love you.
